sentences.text: Mokshany.
But 'Mokrawy' is, is-- in, in English, it would be 'dampness.' Mokrawy.
In Polish, it means something, yes, is damp.
So Mokshany must have been a place where it was very damp around-- perhaps they were damp.
I don't know.
I never was there.
Yes.
Well, I had no white wedding dress because I found it was very funny, those white wedding dresses.
And I could not-- I was still very practical also.
I had really not enough money to have a dress and never wear it again.
But  that was against my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] upbringing.

And so I decided, together with my, my sister-- well, my sister-in-law, my future sister-in-law, Lusia, who was very, very well-dressed always and very-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] knew everything about what has one to wear, and how it should look, and all the makeup which she wanted me to wear and I never wore.
But anyway, coming back to the dress, I didn't want a white dress.
I wanted a dress which I can wear later.
And so my dressmaker [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bought from-- I don't know, from Paris, I suppose-- a very beautiful, light-blue silk.
And it was a, a dress which I  could wear later and wore later quite often.
And with this dress, I had a coat, navy blue-- not really navy, I would say it is perhaps a French blue now-- which was lined with this silk, a hat to match, which I hated.
I always hated hats.
And, well, this hat was very simple because at the wedding, I had a white veil, which my aunt forgot to bring to the wedding.
But she had-- somebody brought it quick and the hat as well.
It was-- that was-- the hat was so made that I could wear it later as an ordinary hat.
And-- but with the veil, it looked very nice.
So that was my wedding dress.
And then I changed into a more sporty dress.
And-- because it was for going away.
It was winter.
And winter in Poland is no joke.
It is cold.
And also, we traveled first class-- I think in a sleeper, even-- to-- first to Warsaw.
I had to have a, a, a warmish dress, not a warm dress, but a warmish dress, not a silk dress.
And during the day, you didn't wear silk dresses.
And I had also a fur coat already-- the-- my first fur coat in my life, a brown one.
I can't remember what the-- what the fur was.
For evening wear, I had a black Persian coat.
So we arrived in Warsaw and had a room booked in the Hotel Bristol, which was the hotel to be in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And again, I was taken around by my husband to see Warsaw, which is a beautiful-- was a beautiful town.
I learned a lot about the buildings, about the palaces, about the kings, about the whatever-- whoever-- all the princes there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He told me a lot about the history of the-- of the town.
I went the first time in my life to a nightclub.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there, I had a very great disappointment.
I was in an evening dress.
I see this evening dress like now, before me, in tulle.
The front was short, and the back was long.
It was all in, in pleats-- very beautiful.
And when I-- when we were settled down in the nightclub and got something to drink-- I didn't drink; and I still don't drink-- I saw a lady coming in exactly the same dress.
And I really-- then I started to cry.
On the whole, I was not the crying kind.
But I had this exclusive dress made-- not that I paid for it-- but I had it made for me and told nobody else will have a dress like that.
And there, on my first outing-- and my evening was completely ruined.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went, naturally, to, to wonderful restaurants.
But we were only four or five days there.
My husband took me also to a shoemaker.
And that I saw the first time in my life, also a new experience, who took the measurements of my feet.
And then he made-- I don't know what you call it in-- last?
A last.
Lasts.
So later, I could just write to him and say I wanted black-laced shoes or, or brown sandals, or whatever.
And he made the dresses-- he made the shoe without seeing me or even seeing.
My husband had his last already made by the very famous [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shoemaker for men.
You don't say shoemaker, I think, or perhaps it was a shoemaker.
It was-- the name was Hiszspanski.
And-- because he had, for years, his shoes made there just by post.
No, I don't think they were Jews.
But I don't know.
But I don't think there was-- the fur maker in Lwow, where I had my first furs made were-- that was a Jewish firm.
That I know.
And also the firm in Lwow where my husband had his silk shirts-- white silk shirts with the monogram 'MS' written.
They were from a firm, [?
Stark, ?] and they were Jewish.
And also, there was a firm with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] writing paper, and, and pens, and what do you call-- stationary.
But they had also very beautiful leather things that was the name of [?
Probst. ?] They were Jewish.
So-- but that was in Lwow.
So coming back to Warsaw, I saw quite a lot.
I was very impressed by everything.
Oh, yes, and I had my pearls knotted because they were not knotted, which-- when I took them to the jeweler to have them, I think, valued for an insurance or something.
I don't know why they were taken, why my husband took me to this jeweller.
And the jeweller saw they are not knotted.
He could not understand pearls of this value not knotted, because if you-- if it breaks, you lose all.
And if you break a knotted, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] knotted-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you call it?
Pearl.
You just lose one.
Then you lose only one.
Anyway, they were-- they were counted.
He signed that there were 129.
So I remember this.
So it must have made a great impression on me if I remember today-- 129 pearls.
And the next day, we came to fetch them.
Naturally, they are much longer now because they were knotted.
So then we left for Cracow.
We-- I forgot the name of the hotel.
It was certainly a very good hotel.
And there I went the first time in my life in a sled, which was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] with two horses.
What do you call it?
There were two horses-- In harness.
Pardon?
Pulling it, yes.
So there were-- there were a sled pulled by two horses.
There was straw on the-- on the bottom of the sled because to keep the feet warm.
Naturally, I was in my fur coat, and the fur hat, and shawl.
And we had fur blankets-- fur covers.
And we went around, around Cracow.
And that was really something which I never, never saw before.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, I saw the Wawel, which was the-- I, I saw a lot.
I saw the very, very famous church of St.
Mary's.
But we stayed there three days and went to Krynica, which was very near.
In Krynica, my husband didn't have a flat because he didn't know that he will marry in the time between September and, and December.
So he had only a studio room next to his surgery, to his x-ray institute.
And so we had two rooms at the very elegant and very expensive hotel and there stayed for the whole winter because it was not easy to get a flat.
It was a spa.
And they were all hotels, and boarding houses, and pensionnats, and villas.
And--
Yes?
What?
Can I start talking?
Well, life in Krynica was rather dull because I didn't speak Polish.
Everybody was very nice to me because they spoke German.
But one day, my husband decided enough is enough and from today, you speak Polish only.
And I didn't--
German.
Is she listening?
No.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, so I had to learn Polish.
I-- but I learned it only by listening.
And slowly, very slowly, I think I  learned a little bit.
Because up till now, I don't speak really good Polish.
That would be-- that would be wrong to say that I speak a good Polish.
I never know the ending of words, whether it's in an I, or it's a Y, or it's an E-I.
But anyway, I started speaking.
And it made life a little bit better.
My husband had his patients.
My husband was a great bridge player.
Everybody played bridge.
We had  a wonderful dog.
I went with the dog for walks.
And my  husband was also a, a very keen skier.
And he wanted me to ski with him.
But I was never one for winter sports.
It was much too cold for me.
I had a wonderful ski outfit made in, in Lwow still.
But I don't know whether I wore it three times.
So he took Rex-- that was our dog-- for skiing.
And Rex was very happy.
And I stayed at home in the warm hotel or with doctors' wives, gossiped a bit.
And the winter season was more or less for three months.
It was December, January, February.
March was nearly the end.
So-- because it was not cold enough for ski sports, there was no ski events.
I remember the international [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hockey [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] games there.
And the Canadian one that I remember-- it-- we were sitting outside.
There was-- there were no holes.
We were sitting outside, again, with hot water bottles on our bellies, and, and fur coats and fur hats in this bitter, bitter cold.
Well, in March, we returned for a few days to Lwow where my husband had a flat-- the same house as his mother and his sister.
It was a very big house.
They had-- he and his brother had a bachelor flat there.
But then it was our flat when he married.
And then we went to Vienna.
And I introduced my husband to my grandmother.
I took my mother and grandmother out and had a very good time.
It was for-- more or less for three weeks or four weeks because my husband got a little bit bored with all these women around him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came back in May and went straight to Krynica for the summer season.
That was-- that is '28, yes.
That is '28.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just a moment-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] yes, it was.
The wedding, the honeymoon, the first season, and we went to Vienna in '28, and came back for the first summer season.
And we had already in a-- while we were there, we found a kind of flat.
It was not a real flat because there were no real flats there, but two rooms with a little kitchen where I could more or less be a house-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a-- not a landlady.
I wasn't a landlady.
A Hausfrau. 'Hausfrau' is a German word.
But anyway, it was-- that was quite nice.
My mother came to visit us because she wanted to know how we-- how we are coping.
I was very glad to see her.
She told me sometimes off because I was very stubborn.
When my husband said something, I didn't want to do it.
And she thought that I should do  as my husband says.
Yeah, but I thought, I will do as I feel like it.
We went with my mother for long walks.
We were sitting on the promenade.

There was always concert, as in all spas are, a very good concert, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and made a little  excursion around-- beautiful excursions in-- with the [NON-ENGLISH], because there were no cars naturally.
And I enjoyed life there.
In autumn, we went back to Lwow, heard that-- no, that was '28.
Yes, and then it was again a season in Krynica in winter.
Nothing special happened.
In '29, I went alone to Vienna.
My husband stayed in Lwow because I think my brother-in-law went to a congress somewhere abroad.
And he took over the work in there.
I went alone.
And I was quite happy to be with all my schoolmates again.
And-- but I was showing off, naturally.
I had the money.
I took my mother-- my mother worked all the time.
I took my mother out and my grandmother.
And then, when I returned in '29 to, to Lwow, I heard that Lusia is pregnant.
And that was the baby.
They waited so very long.
She had the--
That was my sister-in-law.
And she had two stillbirths before and some miscarriages.
So this baby was really very much loved when it arrived in March '30.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I played-- he, he was called-- it was a boy.
He was called George.
While I was in Lwow in spring, I played with him.
I took him for walks.
He had a nanny from Vienna, a [NON-ENGLISH] nanny that was special.
They were allowed only to stay one year with the child-- with the baby and didn't do anything but look after the baby.
Then it was summer again.
And, well, next thing was that I was pregnant.
And there was great jubilation in the Schutz family.
There will be a child.
So I stayed in Krynica in winter till the beginning of January.
And then I  returned to Lwow because Krynica had  no hospital for a birth.
My mother came in-- the baby was due in February.
My mother came middle of-- in-- at the beginning of February.
Jan didn't arrive, didn't arrive.
So she wanted just to go home again because she didn't have her holidays so long.
But then, a cesarean-- it was again a cesarean operation.
Jan arrived a little bit of a small baby, but healthy.
And everything was fine.
After two or three-- he got nearly everything from George because George was a year older.
He didn't need a big pram.
He didn't need a little vest and whatever was needed for a baby.
So I had no problem with that.
We went to, to Krynica back, but decided already that Krynica is not-- no life for us because what, what future is there doing-- going for?
My husband heard about a, a position in Lublin in the Jewish hospital and also in two Jewish centers where they needed an x-ray specialist.
Well, they didn't need in the center.
But they sent people from the center to him to be x-rayed.
And so he went to Lublin.
I stayed with Jan in Lwow.
He got a-- he found a very big, very nice flat.
I came to help to get furniture, and so on.
Jan stayed with the nanny and grandmother and aunt in Lwow.
Then I came for him.
And we settled down in Lublin.
There I had-- at last, I could be a real, [SIGHS] what I called before Hausfrau.
I had a cook.
I had a parlor maid.
I had a nanny.
It was a girl coming as a receptionist for a few hours a day.
And I helped also my husband in the hospital as a physiotherapist-- voluntary, he didn't need that.
They, they were not paid.
Lublin was, for me, an ugly town and a very unpleasant town--
--very Jewish, very Jewish, very Orthodox, which I couldn't stand and didn't understand.
The doctors-- the Polish doctors sent their patients to Polish x-ray specialists.
And the  Jewish doctors sent their patients to the Jewish ones.
So it was always what, with these patients-- I didn't like it very much.
But I made friends with a few people.
And I had one-- I had-- with a Jewish family, the Brombergs, very, very Orthodox, but educated and civilized, not the Jews in the kind of ghetto behind one of the walls.
That-- it was not a wall built by the German.
It's a ghetto wall.
It was old wall.
And behind that, there was the Jewish-- the Jewish district.
And they were really ghetto-like, these Jews were.
But the Brombergs were civilized, educated.
And their daughter was a young widow with a boy of Jan's age.
And we got-- friends.
And Jan and Alek were great friends.
And the two nannies were friends.
So everything was fine.
Life went on till, more or less, it was-- it was winter '38.
No idea.
I just wanted to come to that.
I had no idea.
Not only me-- I was not interested in politics at all.
But what, what about all these educated men, all these doctors, lawyers?
Didn't they know what was going on in the world?
I wasn't interested.
I had my child.
I had my, my cook to instruct what to make for supper.
And I wasn't interested in, in politics.
And-- but we were talking always because-- about moving again because my husband wanted to go to either Warsaw, or, you would laugh, South Africa.
That he wanted.
He didn't want to stay in Lublin.
Lublin was just a second step.

So when-- through my family, we heard that a quite well-known x-ray specialist would like to find a partner who has money and good instruments or apparats, or whatever you call this-- equipment-- he would like to have a big practice in Warsaw.
So my husband went to Warsaw, had consultations and talks with this doctor.
I think his name was Levinson.
But I'm not sure.
So let's call him Dr.
L.
And my husband agreed to move to Warsaw.
And that was spring '38.
So he-- they-- he and Dr.
L found, in Warsaw, a very big flat which could be-- there would be the institute installed.
And [SIGHS] so we moved again.
And before we moved, my friend Renia, with the little boy, moved also to Warsaw because her brother was there.
And she had friends.
She was a social worker, but a Jewish-- she was Jewish-- a Jewish social worker.
And so I was quite glad that when I come to Warsaw, I will have a friend, not only family, because I didn't think very much of my family, as a matter of fact.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The plan was-- and that was spring '38-- spring '39 already.
So the Austrian-- so the Germans were already--
They-- my mother--
--was already in Lwow.
My mother moved with the firm because Dr.
Kreisberg was a Polish citizen.
And he had his headquarters in Lwow.
So my mother moved from Vienna.
My grandmother died in the meantime.
And she moved with two suitcases and, I think, 400 shillings to Lwow, where her family got her, in the meantime, a little flat.
And she settled down quite nicely.
She even visited us in Lwow-- in Lublin and heard about our plans to go to Warsaw.
And she was quite for it because Lublin was not really the place to be.
So in, in spring '39, all that happened-- this moving again.
We found also a flat in Zoliborz, which was a kind of like Chiswick in, in London, a district of Warsaw.
We found a flat which was just being built.
There were no new buildings getting up.
And I looked after the arrangements there.
My husband looked after the installment-- the institution-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] institute.
And Jan was in a, a school camp near Warsaw.
It was the beginning of the school holidays.
We found also a very good school for Jan that was-- the cause also to move because Lublin had no schools for Jewish children.
A higher school was-- they, they were not accepted, Jewish children.
So-- and we lived in two rooms, rented rooms, went out for meals.
There was no question, I suppose.
Breakfast I  made at home.
And one day, we got a telephone-- a telegram that Milek, my brother-in-law, had a heart attack and could Max come and help out before-- till they find somebody to take over.
So he left me in charge of everything and went to Lwow.
And that was-- must have been July.
After two or three weeks-- Jan was very happy in this camp, in the summer camp with all his friends and very, very nice.
I decided to visit Max and my mother in Lwow for a weekend or for a few days, and then come back to Warsaw and see what's going on.
While I was in in Lwow, Max got his call-up papers because he was in the reserve-- a captain in the reserve.
And that was the end of our dreams.
Also, we thought that there won't be a war.
He was just called up.
But I wanted to have Jan with me.
And civilians were not allowed to travel anymore.
The trains were all taken up by the services.
So Jan, Jan-- Max went to fetch Jan, brought him back.
As-- the child was standing in, in shorts, and the little shirts, or whatever he had, sandals-- just grabbed him and came back to Lwow.
And we stayed with my mother.
Max had to go back to his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unit.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 17th of September, the Russian moved into, into Lwow.
My husband was already gone with his unit.
We didn't know where.
And I was with my mother and Jan in my mother's flat.
But the Russian-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in-- I thought it was terrible.
But later, in comparison with the Germans, they were angels for us, not for other people who were killed in, in Siberia.
The first personal loss for us was the arrest and deportation of Victor Hyas, who was my mother-in-law's brother.
He was the president of B'nai B'rith, the vice-- of-- the vice president of Lwow, and a banker.
And that was, for the Russians, enough to kill  a person.
He was deported and killed in Kiev after a few days in prison.
That was the first personal loss for us.
Then the Russian told my mother to leave her flat.
That means me and Jan also.
But we got two rooms in a very big flat with other families.
So that we had two rooms and had to share the bathroom and the kitchen with two or three other families.
But--
Oh, yes, they were Jewish families.
